content:
{
  "title": "Prompt Calibration via Consistency Probes for LLM Uncertainty Quantification",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident answers, even when unsure, and their output probabilities are poorly calibrated. Reliable uncertainty quantification is essential for trustworthy LLM deployment but remains challenging with current prompting techniques.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly used in critical domains (like healthcare and law), miscalibrated confidence can lead to high-stakes errors. Recent work shows LLMs’ confidence scores poorly align with correctness, especially on out-of-distribution or ambiguous inputs. Improved prompt-based calibration could enhance safety and downstream decision-making without retraining models.",
  "method": "We propose Consistency Probing Prompts (CPP): for each input, generate several semantically equivalent rephrasings or paraphrases using a controlled prompt. Query the LLM with each version, collect the predicted answers and their probabilities, and use the agreement (consistency) across paraphrases as a prompt-level uncertainty score. We further prompt the model to rate its own confidence per response, combining self-rated and consistency-based signals into a calibrated uncertainty estimate. This method leverages LLMs’ own linguistic flexibility and metacognitive capabilities, requiring no model retraining or architectural changes.",
  "experiment_plan": "Evaluate CPP on QA datasets (e.g., TruthfulQA, MMLU, SQuAD) and factuality-sensitive benchmarks. Compare against probability-only and baseline prompting for calibration, using metrics such as Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier score, and selective accuracy. Assess performance on both in-domain and distribution-shifted data to test generalization."
}
